1: import test from 'node:test'
2: import assert from 'node:assert/strict'
3: import { addItem, createCartState } from './src/useCartState.mjs'
4: 
5: test('addItem returns a new state object without mutating previous state', () => {
6:   const state = createCartState()
7:   const next = addItem(state, { id: 'a', label: 'Alpha' })
8:   assert.notEqual(next, state)
9:   assert.deepEqual(state.items, [])
10:   assert.equal(state.total, 0)
11:   assert.deepEqual(next.items, [{ id: 'a', label: 'Alpha' }])
12:   assert.equal(next.total, 1)
13: })
</content>
state.title: bench-targets/react-state/useCartState.test.mjs

part.text:
The bug is in `addItem`: it reassigns `next = state` (line 6), so `next.items` is the same reference as `state.items`. When `next.items.push(item)` is called, it mutates the original state.

I'll create a shallow copy of the state object to prevent mutation:

input.description: Run the test to verify the bug fix

state.title: Run the test to verify the bug fix

part.text:
Test passes: `addItem returns a new state object without mutating previous state`. 

Fix summary: Changed `next = state` to `next = { ...state, items: [...state.items] }` to properly create a new array for items instead of mutating the original one.
